narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60—above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 33, you've shown real academic potential that many law schools value. Your family comes from a middle-income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which aligns with the vast majority of students in the dataset at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 46% probability that you would fail the bar on your first try—high enough to trigger a rejection of your application. The primary driver pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers who typically pass the bar. This factor had the strongest influence, outweighing your other strengths and tipping the scales toward concern about your bar exam readiness. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your undergraduate GPA, which strongly pulled the prediction in a positive direction toward passing, as it's notably higher than average and reflects consistent excellence. Your LSAT score also helped, nudging the odds toward success despite being a bit under the 36.70 average, showing the model recognized your testing ability as a favorable sign. Overall, while your early Year 1 decile of 3 was middling, it was your sharp drop in Year 3 performance that dominated the decision, making the model view your trajectory as riskier than most applicants who get approved. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile is strong in GPA and enrollment status but hampered by that late-grade dip, which stands out as unusual and influential. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-class background—many like you succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on boosting your recent academic decile through graduate-level courses or a post-bac program, retake the LSAT if feasible to hit average or above, and gather strong letters highlighting your upward momentum. Reapplying after addressing that Year 3 weakness could flip the model's view, as even small shifts in key areas often lead to approvals. You're close—many students in similar spots turn it around with persistence.
